topics.name: myocardial infarction
question_patterns: What causes myocardial infarction?
What causes a heart attack?
Why does myocardial infarction happen?
topics.answer: Myocardial infarction can be caused by atherosclerosis that leads to coronary artery blockage and reduced blood flow to heart tissue.
topics.explanation: In this rule-based example, atherosclerosis contributes to plaque build-up in the coronary arteries. This can narrow or block the artery, reduce blood flow, and deprive heart muscle of oxygen, which may lead to myocardial infarction.

topics.name: hypertension
question_patterns: What causes hypertension?
Why does high blood pressure happen?
What leads to elevated blood pressure?
topics.answer: Hypertension can result from increased vascular resistance and fluid retention, which raise pressure inside the arteries.
topics.explanation: In this simplified cardiology example, narrowing or stiffness of blood vessels increases vascular resistance. Fluid retention can also increase blood volume. Together, these changes raise arterial pressure and contribute to hypertension.

topics.name: heart failure
question_patterns: What causes heart failure?
Why does the heart fail to pump well?
What leads to reduced cardiac output?
topics.answer: Heart failure can occur when damaged or weakened heart muscle reduces the heart's ability to pump blood effectively.
topics.explanation: In this simplified example, myocardial damage weakens cardiac contraction. Reduced contractility lowers cardiac output, which can lead to symptoms and clinical findings associated with heart failure.

topics.name: angina
question_patterns: What causes angina?
Why does angina cause chest pain?
What causes cardiac chest pain?
topics.answer: Angina is commonly caused by reduced oxygen supply to heart muscle, often due to narrowed coronary arteries.
topics.explanation: In this rule-based example, coronary artery narrowing limits blood flow during increased demand. The resulting oxygen mismatch in the heart muscle produces ischemia, which manifests as chest discomfort known as angina.

topics.name: arrhythmia
question_patterns: What causes arrhythmia?
Why does an irregular heartbeat happen?
What disrupts heart rhythm?
topics.answer: Arrhythmia can be caused by abnormal electrical signaling that disrupts the heart's normal rhythm.
topics.explanation: In this simplified example, changes in electrical conduction alter the timing of cardiac activation. This disrupts coordinated heartbeats and can produce an arrhythmia.

topics.name: atherosclerosis
question_patterns: What causes atherosclerosis?
Why does plaque build up in arteries?
What leads to hardening of the arteries?
topics.answer: Atherosclerosis develops when lipids, inflammatory cells, and fibrous tissue accumulate in artery walls and form plaques.
topics.explanation: In this simplified model, endothelial injury and lipid deposition start plaque formation. Inflammation and fibrous tissue growth enlarge the plaque, which can stiffen and narrow the artery.

topics.name: coronary artery disease
question_patterns: What causes coronary artery disease?
Why do coronary arteries become narrowed?
What leads to coronary artery disease?
topics.answer: Coronary artery disease is usually caused by atherosclerotic plaque that narrows coronary arteries and limits blood flow to the heart.
topics.explanation: In this rule-based example, plaque forms in the coronary arteries. As plaque grows, the vessel lumen narrows and coronary blood flow becomes limited, which can cause ischemia and related symptoms.

topics.name: cardiac arrest
question_patterns: What causes cardiac arrest?
Why does the heart suddenly stop?
What leads to sudden cardiac arrest?
topics.answer: Cardiac arrest can occur when a severe electrical disturbance prevents the heart from pumping blood effectively.
topics.explanation: In this simplified example, a dangerous rhythm such as ventricular fibrillation disrupts coordinated contraction. Without effective pumping, circulation stops and cardiac arrest occurs.

topics.name: valvular heart disease
question_patterns: What causes valvular heart disease?
Why do heart valves fail?
What causes valve stenosis or regurgitation?
topics.answer: Valvular heart disease can occur when heart valves become narrowed, leaky, or damaged, disrupting normal blood flow through the heart.
topics.explanation: In this simplified model, valve damage or degeneration changes valve structure. A narrowed valve obstructs forward flow, while a leaky valve allows backward flow. Either pattern can increase cardiac workload over time.

topics.name: cardiomyopathy
question_patterns: What causes cardiomyopathy?
Why does the heart muscle become abnormal?
What leads to enlarged or thickened heart muscle?
topics.answer: Cardiomyopathy can result from structural or functional abnormalities of the heart muscle that reduce effective pumping.
topics.explanation: In this simplified example, genetic factors, chronic pressure overload, ischemia, or toxins can alter the myocardium. The heart muscle may become enlarged, thickened, or weakened, which can impair pumping function.

topics.name: myocarditis
question_patterns: What causes myocarditis?
Why does the heart muscle become inflamed?
What leads to myocardial inflammation?
topics.answer: Myocarditis can result from viral, autoimmune, or toxic triggers that inflame the heart muscle and impair its contractile function.
topics.explanation: In this simplified example, a viral infection or autoimmune reaction activates an immune response in the myocardium. Tissue inflammation and edema develop in the heart muscle and impair contraction, producing myocarditis.

topics.name: pericarditis
question_patterns: What causes pericarditis?
Why does the pericardium become inflamed?
What leads to pericardial inflammation?
topics.answer: Pericarditis can occur when the pericardium becomes inflamed, often following an infection, autoimmune trigger, or recent cardiac injury.
topics.explanation: In this simplified example, an infectious or autoimmune trigger activates the immune response in the pericardium. Inflammation of the pericardial sac can produce a friction rub and sometimes a pericardial effusion, which together describe pericarditis.

topics.name: atrial fibrillation
question_patterns: What causes atrial fibrillation?
Why are P waves absent in atrial fibrillation?
What causes an irregularly irregular rhythm?
What does ECG evidence of atrial fibrillation indicate?
topics.answer: Atrial fibrillation is an arrhythmia in which disorganized atrial electrical activity produces ineffective atrial contraction and an irregularly irregular ventricular rhythm.
topics.explanation: In this simplified topic, atrial enlargement or electrical irritability creates multiple atrial reentry wavelets. The atria fibrillate instead of contracting effectively, P waves may be absent on ECG, and ventricular response becomes irregular.
reasoning_path: Absent P waves and irregular rhythm

topics.name: infective endocarditis
question_patterns: What causes infective endocarditis?
Why do vegetations form on heart valves?
What causes fever and a new murmur after bacteremia?
What does endocarditis on a valve mean?
topics.answer: Infective endocarditis occurs when microbes seed the endocardium or heart valves and form infected vegetations that can damage valves and embolize.
topics.explanation: In this simplified topic, bacteremia allows organisms to adhere to damaged endocardium or valve surfaces. Platelet-fibrin deposition and microbial growth produce vegetations, which can cause fever, a murmur, valve dysfunction, or embolic findings.

topics.name: aortic stenosis
question_patterns: What causes aortic stenosis?
What does a harsh systolic murmur radiating to the carotids suggest?
Why does a narrowed aortic valve cause syncope?
What valve lesion causes crescendo-decrescendo murmur?
topics.answer: Aortic stenosis is narrowing of the aortic valve that obstructs left ventricular outflow and can produce a systolic ejection murmur.
topics.explanation: In this simplified topic, valve calcification or congenital bicuspid valve anatomy narrows the aortic valve opening. Outflow obstruction increases left ventricular pressure load and can produce exertional symptoms and a characteristic murmur.
reasoning_path: Aortic valve calcification or bicuspid valve

topics.name: mitral regurgitation
question_patterns: What causes mitral regurgitation?
What does a holosystolic murmur at the apex suggest?
Why does mitral valve leakage cause left atrial volume overload?
What valve disease causes blood to flow back into the left atrium?
topics.answer: Mitral regurgitation occurs when the mitral valve does not close completely, allowing blood to leak from the left ventricle into the left atrium during systole.
topics.explanation: In this simplified topic, leaflet, chordal, papillary muscle, or annular dysfunction prevents complete mitral valve closure. Systolic backflow creates volume overload and a characteristic holosystolic murmur.

topics.name: mitral valve prolapse
question_patterns: What causes mitral valve prolapse?
What does a midsystolic click suggest?
Why does a redundant mitral leaflet prolapse into the left atrium?
What valve condition causes a click followed by late systolic murmur?
topics.answer: Mitral valve prolapse occurs when redundant or myxomatous mitral valve leaflets bulge into the left atrium during systole, sometimes causing mitral regurgitation.
topics.explanation: In this simplified topic, myxomatous leaflet change and chordal redundancy allow the mitral leaflet to bow backward during systole. This can produce a midsystolic click and sometimes a late systolic murmur if regurgitation occurs.
reasoning_path: Midsystolic click or late systolic murmur

topics.name: patent ductus arteriosus
question_patterns: What causes patent ductus arteriosus?
What does a continuous machine-like murmur suggest?
Why does PDA cause left-to-right shunting?
What congenital lesion leaves the ductus arteriosus open?
topics.answer: Patent ductus arteriosus is persistence of the fetal ductus arteriosus, allowing abnormal blood flow between the aorta and pulmonary artery after birth.
topics.explanation: In this simplified topic, failure of ductus arteriosus closure leaves a connection between systemic and pulmonary circulation. A left-to-right shunt increases pulmonary blood flow and can create a continuous machine-like murmur.

topics.name: tetralogy of Fallot
question_patterns: What causes tetralogy of Fallot?
What congenital heart disease causes cyanotic spells?
Why does right ventricular outflow obstruction cause cyanosis in TOF?
What condition has VSD, overriding aorta, pulmonary stenosis, and right ventricular hypertrophy?
topics.answer: Tetralogy of Fallot is a cyanotic congenital heart condition involving right ventricular outflow obstruction, ventricular septal defect, overriding aorta, and right ventricular hypertrophy.
topics.explanation: In this simplified topic, malalignment of the outflow tract produces pulmonary outflow obstruction and a VSD. Deoxygenated blood can shunt right-to-left, producing cyanosis and classic TOF findings.
reasoning_path: Right ventricular outflow obstruction with VSD

topics.name: coarctation of the aorta
question_patterns: What causes coarctation of the aorta?
Why does coarctation cause upper extremity hypertension?
What congenital lesion causes weak femoral pulses?
What causes rib notching with arm-leg blood pressure difference?
topics.answer: Coarctation of the aorta is congenital narrowing of the aorta that obstructs flow to the lower body and can cause upper-extremity hypertension with weak lower-extremity pulses.
topics.explanation: In this simplified topic, a narrowed aortic segment increases resistance proximal to the narrowing and reduces distal perfusion. This can produce arm-leg blood pressure differences, weak femoral pulses, and collateral vessel changes.

topics.name: pulmonary embolism
question_patterns: What causes pulmonary embolism?
Why does a deep vein thrombosis cause sudden dyspnea?
What causes acute pleuritic chest pain with hypoxemia?
What happens when an embolus blocks pulmonary arteries?
topics.answer: Pulmonary embolism occurs when thrombotic material, often from a deep vein thrombosis, travels to the pulmonary arteries and obstructs pulmonary blood flow.
topics.explanation: In this simplified topic, a venous thrombus embolizes to the pulmonary circulation. Pulmonary artery obstruction increases dead-space ventilation, can lower oxygenation, and may strain the right ventricle.
